titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Victoria Mary Tyrrell 25, July, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: William Upton Tyrrell is experiencing rain at the from and describes the sound of artillery fire as "artificial" lightening. This letter also discusses matters at home.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
25 July 1917 My dear Minnie No time to write in french today and
besides it would be very difficult as
there are lots of fellows round all
talking to me at the same time. Well
by the time you get this you and
Bobby will be home and I am sure
greatly delighted at it. We moved yesterday night back about
10 miles and are again in tents. We
had a distinctly lively time in the last
place as a lot of queer things used to
drop near and make a noise when
they dropped too. I wonder what they
could have been. We were very
lucky as none actually came into the
camp. We were out all night on
working parties. Today it is raining very heavilIy
worse luck. I hope that it will not
continue but it has all the appearance
of it. We can hear two sorts of
thunder, real and artificial. The people in this country are not
nearly so friendly as over the border
They never want to give us
anything or help in any way. They
sometimes take the handle off the
pumps but of course we soon
stop that. However we get on A1
and enjoy ourselves quite well. Yesterday the dust was awful
when we were marching. Today it
is mud. I hope the weather is
not going to break now after
all the fine weather we had. We have not had any bad rain
since early April now. At a good
hour be it spoken! The books in the last parcel
were A1 and most useful as you
see we just read all day when not
asleep. When is Mr Bookey coming down
to see you. It will be great if you
get a clown also
I suppose she is nice and young too
isnt it a pity I am not there? I think Mrs Hone must be at the
Curragh by now as she said the
middle of July. Write and ask her
you know her permanent address
Carrinhaunes House, Carrinhaunes Co
Dublin. I have been thinking about it
and really it would be easier for
her to get down from Dublin as it would
be a very long drive. She was only
to stay at Curragh for 6 weeks. I am
writing to her today. I suppose you will be a regular old
woman now and put up your
bunch of tow in a knot since you
have left school. No news but will write soon
again but not every day as I have
nothing to write about. With very best love to all from
Your most affec brother
Willie
